BLUE ORIGIN AIMS TO LAND NEXT NEW GLENN BOOSTER, THEN REUSE IT FOR MOON MISSION
articles.summary: The second launch of Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket will send two NASA-funded satellites toward Mars to study the processes that drove the planet's evolution from a warmer, wetter world to the cold, dry planet of today. A successful launch would nudge Blue Origin closer to winning certification from the Space Force to begin launching national security satellites. The company plans to recover the New Glenn rocket's first stage booster during the mission.

articles.title: OPENAI'S SORA SOARS TO NO. 3 ON THE US APP STORE
articles.summary: OpenAI's Sora app saw 56,000 downloads on its first day. It is now ranked third Top Overall app on the US App Store, despite being invite-only and limited to users in the US and Canada. The ChatGPT app and Google's Gemini iOS apps had stronger launches, with each reaching at least 80,000 downloads on day one, but since Sora is invite-only, that may not be a fair comparison. The strong release indicates there is a demand for AI video tools with a social networking-like experience.

articles.title: DEMYSTIFYING THE SCIENCE BEHIND FISSION AND FUSION
articles.summary: Kemmerer, Wyoming, will soon be home to the most advanced nuclear facility in the world. The first-ever Natrium plant, it will bring safe, next-generation nuclear energy. The plant was designed by TerraPower, a company Bill Gates started in 2008. This article looks at the science behind the Natrium plant. TerraPower aims to bring the plant online in 2030.

articles.title: THE QUEST TO SEQUENCE THE GENOMES OF EVERYTHING
articles.summary: The Earth BioGenome Project (EBP) aims to obtain a genome sequence for every named species of eukaryotic organism on the planet by 2035. Its success will depend on researchers' ability to scale several biotech technologies. Specialists on the front lines of the genomic revolution in biology are confident that such scaling will be possible. Having a genome sequence for every eukaryotic creature will give researchers insights into how evolution proceeded for a myriad of life forms. It could lead to the discovery and development of many drugs, enzymes, catalysts, and other chemicals of incalculable value.

articles.title: HOW GOOGLE, MICROSOFT, OPENAI, AND SERVICENOW ARE THINKING ABOUT AI AGENT SECURITY
articles.summary: Find out what the world's AI leaders are doing about the most urgent security challenge. The AI Agent Security Summit (Oct 8 / San Francisco) [9] packs _a lot_ into one day: 3 keynotes, 6 lightning talks, and 2 panels; featuring security experts from OWASP, Glean, ScaleAI, Stanford and more. Seats are limited (literally). Save yours

articles.title: ASKED TO DO SOMETHING ILLEGAL AT WORK? HERE'S WHAT THESE SOFTWARE ENGINEERS DID
articles.summary: Nishad Singh had confirmation that something illegal was happening at FTX, but he stayed, and now he is facing up to 75 years in jail. A senior engineering member at Pollen is now at the mercy of the UK police with what could be a potential wire fraud case after being asked to run a script that double-charged customers (and then doing so). The Director of Engineering at Frank, a student loan startup, flat out refused to create a fake set of data to pump up customer numbers. Their refusal is what makes them the only example on this list that is totally legally safe.

articles.title: WHERE IT'S AT://
articles.summary: All servers speaking the AT protocol are part of 'the atmosphere', a web of hyperlinked JSON. Each piece of JSON in the atmosphere has its own at://URI. This post looks at the exact process of resolving an at:// URI. The AT protocol is fundamentally an abstraction over HTTP, DNS, and JSON. It turns the web into a place where content belongs to its creators, not to the apps that display it, by putting users in an authority position, separating identity from hosting, and making data portable.

articles.title: IN A SEA OF TECH TALENT, COMPANIES CAN'T FIND THE WORKERS THEY WANT
articles.summary: Many tech companies are saying that good help is hard to find. While there is an ample supply of eager and capable engineers for hire, employers say there aren't enough people with the most in-demand skills. Companies are looking for AI-specific experience, but relatively few people have it because the technology is so new. Companies should focus on identifying candidates with transferable skills and let those people learn on the job.

articles.title: META TIGHTENS GRIP ON AI RESEARCH, SPARKING INTERNAL ANGER AND FEARS FOR OPEN CULTURE
articles.summary: Meta has added a new review process for academic papers, sparking internal anger. Yann LeCun, a Turing Award winner and a pivotal figure in modern AI, reportedly considered resigning over the change. The new policy is aimed at aligning pure research more closely with Meta's product road map. It will also help mitigate potential reputational risks from controversial findings. The constant state of reorganization raises critical questions about whether Meta is able to turn its recently acquired assets into a dominant force.

articles.title: ALPHABET PLANS SALE, SPINOFF OF VERILY UNIT, EXECUTIVE SAYS
articles.summary: Verily, a sister company to Google, develops software and services to help health practitioners advance research and improve patient care.

articles.title: NINE HTTP EDGE CASES EVERY API DEVELOPER SHOULD UNDERSTAND
articles.summary: Most security vulnerabilities come from unnecessary custom code that reimplements what frameworks already do correctly.

articles.title: OPENAI WRAPS $6.6 BILLION SHARE SALE AT $500 BILLION VALUATION
articles.summary: The share sale allowed current and former employees to sell stock.

articles.title: ANTI-AGING BREAKTHROUGH: STEM CELLS REVERSE SIGNS OF AGING IN MONKEYS
articles.summary: Senescence-resistant stem cells, which exhibit greater resistance to age-related stress, are a promising solution when it comes to combating degenerative aging.

articles.title: PATIENTS ARE DIAGNOSING THEMSELVES WITH HOME TESTS, DEVICES, AND CHATBOTS
articles.summary: Patient are now shouldering responsibility for diagnosing their own symptoms, tracking their own medical data, and ordering their own lab tests.

articles.title: AI IS RESHAPING CHILDHOOD IN CHINA
articles.summary: AI tools, from robot toys to homework-grading systems, are flooding classrooms and households in China, providing learning materials along with companionship and emotional support.
